S1Z Lab 5 1 Welcome to Lab 5 1.1 Learning Outcomes 1.2 Introduction 1.3 Getting Started

S1Z Lab 5 1 Welcome to Lab 5 1.1 Learning Outcomes By the end of this computer lab you should be able to: Use R to produce plots of report quality. Use R to produce autocorrelation function plots. Use R to perform differencing. Interpret R output for linear models to describe linear trends and seasonal patterns. Plot and interpret spatial data in R. 1.2 Introduction In today's lab, we will use R to explore temporal patterns in lake surface water temperature (LSWT) at Lake Balaton in Hungary. Figure 1.1: Balaton lake, in Hungary, is the largest lake in Central Europe with a surface area of 600 km\(^2\). Image source: GoogleMaps We will work through two examples together and then you can produce a similar set of analyses for an alternative lake as a Group Exercise at the end of the Lab. 1.3 Getting Started Firstly, make sure you have the geoR package installed and loaded in the R environment. Next, you should download the dataset LakeBalaton.csv from the course Moodle page, and set the working directory to where your dataset is saved. Lake surface water temperature for this lake is available in the .csv file LakeBalaton.csv now stored into your working directory. To open this data file in R you can use the read.csv function as shown below. This will assign the data to the object balaton. install.packages("geoR") library(geoR) # modify this code setwd("path to where the LakeBalaton.csv file is!") balaton <- read.csv("LakeBalaton.csv")
patterns-over-time.html
2 Patterns Over Time 2.1 Surface Water Temperature 2.2 Naming Columns 2.3 Decimal Date 2.4 Plotting The Data

2 Patterns Over Time 2.1 Surface Water Temperature For a set of lakes around the world, lake surface water temperature (LSWT) has been obtained using reflectance measurements recorded by the AATSR satellite system. We have a set of monthly LSWT values for Lake Balaton, a large freshwater lake located in Hungary. Note that the observed series has been reconstructed so that it is possible for LSWT below zero to be recorded. Look at the first few lines of the data frame by running the following code: head(balaton) This worksheet contains 4 columns, described as follows: C1 Year C2 Month (1= Jan, ..., 12= Dec) C3 Season (1= Mar-May, 2= Jun-Aug, 3= Sept-Nov, 4= Dec-Feb) C4 Lake surface water temperature (degrees Celsius) Column names can be seen by running the following line of code: names(balaton) The whole dataset can be viewed by typing the name of the object in the script file and running that line: balaton 2.2 Naming Columns We are going to extract and name columns from the data set so they can be easily referred to later on. Run the following lines of code: year <- balaton[,1] month <- balaton[,2] lswt <- balaton[,4] year # this allows you to check what the contents of the object created 2.3 Decimal Date Decimal date provides a continuous measure of time which is useful for producing plots. To construct decimal date we need to create a new variable, say dec.date, as per the following formula: \[\mathrm{dec.date} = \mathrm{year}+\frac{\mathrm{month}-1}{12} \] 2.3.1 Task 1 Write code to create a new variable called dec.date from the variables year and month. Hint You may want to write the above formula in R. Solution dec.date <- year + (month-1)/12 You can check your variable by simply running 'dec.date' in your R script, after having created the variable. 2.4 Plotting The Data For a single lake, the pattern over time for LSWT can be examined by plotting the data. (We first need to add the newly created 'dec.date' varaible to the data set). rename(balaton, lswt = BALATON) # Renaming the variable in the data set from it's original BALATON to lswt balaton <- balaton %>% mutate(dec.date = year + (month-1)/12) # Adding the dec.date variable ggplot(data = balaton, aes(x = dec.date, y = lswt)) + geom_line() + labs(title = "LSWT (Celsius) at Lake Balaton") + xlab("Date") + ylab("LSWT (Celcius)") # Creating a line plot using the tidyverse packages and our variables Which of the following sentences best describes the LSWT patterns over time at Lake Balaton? Evidence for a seasonal pattern but no evidence for a trend Evidence for both trend and seasonal pattern No evidence for regular seasonal pattern Evidence for an increasing trend
seasonal-patterns.html
3 Seasonal Patterns 3.1 Task 2 3.2 Task 3

3 Seasonal Patterns In addition to looking at trend over time it is also of interest to examine the seasonal pattern within each year. 3.1 Task 2 Write code to produce a seasonal plot of the LSWT. Hint You may want to copy the previous code for exploring the patterns over time but use the month variable instead, and change any other necessary code. You will also want to produce a scatter plot as opposed to a line graph. Solution ggplot(data = balaton, aes(x = month, y = lswt)) + geom_point() + labs(title = "Monthly LSWT (Celsius) at Lake Balaton") + xlab("Month") + ylab("LSWT (Celcius)") 3.2 Task 3 Write code to produce a boxplot of the LSWT by month Hint You may want to use 'geom_boxplot' instead of 'geom_line'. Solution ggplot(data = balaton, aes(x = as.factor(month), y = lswt)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Monthly LSWT (Celsius) at Lake Balaton") + xlab("Month") + ylab("LSWT (Celcius)") Boxplots enable us to see a summary of the LSWT measurements for each month, and they also make it easy to compare the distribution and variability of lswt across the months. In which months are the lowest temperatures observed? December - January November - December January - February July - August
serial-correlation.html
4 Serial Correlation

4 Serial Correlation In order to investigate any temporal trend in LSWT, a linear regression model could be fitted, however this model assumes that the observations are independent. This is often not the case in environmental data due to the presence of serial correlation. To investigate serial correlation, the sample autocorrelation function (ACF) can be used. The sample ACF is the collection of sample correlation coefficients that correspond to the cross-correlation of the data with itself at a series of different lags in time. It was defined in lectures as: \[r(k)=\frac{\sum_{t=k+1}^{n} (x_t-\bar{x})(x_{t-k}-\bar{x})}{\sum_{t=1}^n(x_t-\bar{x})^2} \] To obtain a sample ACF plot in R, we can use the acf function: acf(lswt, main="ACF of LSWT at Lake Balaton", xlab="Lag (months)") What does the ACF indicate? the presence of autocorrelation the absence of autocorrelation Looking at the ACF, what is the period of the seasonal pattern in the time series? (In months).
modelling-trend-and-seasonality.html
5 Modelling Trend and Seasonality 5.1 Recap: The Harmonic Regression Model 5.2 Fitting a Linear Model in R 5.3 Assumption Checking 5.4 Plot Data with Fitted Curve

5 Modelling Trend and Seasonality 5.1 Recap: The Harmonic Regression Model During the case study lectures, we have looked at describing trend and seasonal patterns using linear regression models. We have considered how to fit a linear model through estimating the parameters in a harmonic regression model like the following: \[Y_t= \beta_0+\beta_1x_t+\gamma_1 \text{sin}\left(\frac{2\pi m_t}{p}\right)+\gamma_2 \text{cos}\left(\frac{2\pi m_t}{p}\right)+\epsilon_t\] where: \(Y_t\) corresponds to the response variable, in this example, LSWT \(x_t\) corresponds to the covariate or explanatory variable decimal date, dec.date \(\gamma_1\) and \(\gamma_2\) are the coefficients of the harmonic terms describing the seasonal pattern \(p\) is the fixed period of the seasonal pattern \(m_t\) is the month of the year. This linear model is based on several assumptions: The random errors, \(\epsilon\), are independent. The random errors, \(\epsilon\), have mean zero. The random errors, \(\epsilon\), follow a normal distribution. The random errors, \(\epsilon\), have constant variance. The first assumption will be discussed later. Assumptions 2-4 can be assessed using residual plots. 5.2 Fitting a Linear Model in R 5.2.1 Model with trend and seasonal pattern To find the equation that best describes the LSWT temporal patterns, we use the linear model lm function and store the model output in an object called model1. # fit the model model1 <- lm(lswt ~ dec.date + sin((2*pi*month)/12)+ cos((2*pi*month)/12)) # show the model output summary(model1) This fits a linear regression model with the response variable LSWT and explanatory terms corresponding to trend and seasonal patterns. 5.3 Assumption Checking Before assessing the fit of the model it is important that we check that the assumptions underlying the model are satisfied. In order to do this we need to obtain the residuals after fitting the model above. These can be obtained and stored using the commands: # obtain residuals of model1 res <- residuals(model1) # obtain standardised residuals of model1 stres <- rstandard(model1) with the latter command providing the standardised residuals. Fitted values can also be extracted from the model and stored in an object using the command: # obtain fitted values for model1 fits <- fitted(model1) These commands can be used to produce a plot of the residuals versus the fitted values and a normal probability plot of the residuals to graphically assess assumptions 2,3 and 4. 5.3.1 Standardised residuals versus fitted values 5.3.1.1 Task 4 Write code to produce a scatterplot for the standardized residuals versus the fitted values of model1. Hint Let A denote the event that a person has two differently coloured eyes. Remember that you can use the following formula for computing the probability of seeing at least one person with that characteristic in a sample of size n: \(1-(1-P(A))^n\) Solution ggplot() + geom_point(aes(x = fits, y = stres)) + labs(title = "Residuals Vs Fits for Model 1") + xlab("Fitted Values") + ylab("Standardised Residuals") A normal probability plot of the model residuals can be produced by: qqnorm(stres, main= "Normal Probability Plot of the Residuals for Model 1") qqline(stres, col=2, lwd=2) In the second line of the above code chunk, col sets the colour of the line and lwd sets the line width. Do the model assumptions of zero mean and constant variance errors appear to hold from above scatterplot of the residuals against the fitted values? No Yes Do the residuals appear to be normally distributed? Yes No For the first assumption we can look at an ACF of the residuals. 5.3.1.2 Task 5 Write code to produce an ACF for the standardized residuals of model1. Hint You may want to use the function acf(). Solution acf(stres, main="ACF of Residuals from Model 1") Do the residuals appear to be autocorrelated? No Yes After assessing the assumptions we will now look at a summary of this linear model which can be obtained by typing: summary(model1) Is there any evidence that the LSWT exhibits a linear trend? Yes No Is there any evidence that the LSWT exhibit significant seasonality? No Yes 5.3.2 Model with seasonal pattern only As the trend term is not significant we can remove this term and re-fit the model. 5.3.2.1 Task 6 Write code to fit a model for describing the seasonal patterns of LSWT. Hint You may want to use the function lm() we used before to fit model1 but drop the dec.date term. Solution model2 <- lm(lswt ~ sin((2*pi*month)/12) + cos((2*pi*month)/12)) 5.3.2.2 Task 7 Write code to obtain a summary for model2. Hint You may want to use the function summary(). Solution summary(model2) Both coefficients of the harmonic regression term have p-values <0.05, indicating that there is a statistically significant seasonal pattern. There are now no terms in this model which are not statistically significant. Despite removing the trend term from the model, the R squared adjusted value indicates that the percentage of variability in LSWT explained by the model has not changed and is still 92%. The estimated average value of LSWT in June is (To 2 decimal places). . We should again check that the assumptions are valid for this model - Ill leave this up to you! Start by tracking down the commands required to calculate the standardised residuals, the fitted values and make appropriate plots to check the assumptions (scatterplots, QQ plot, acf plot). # Calculate residuals, standardised residuals and fitted values based on model2 # res2 <- # stres2 <- # fits2 <- # Produce required plots to check assumptions Hint 1 # produce residuals, standardised residuals and fitted values res2 <- residuals(model2) stres2 <- rstandard(model2) fits2 <- fitted(model2) Hint 2 # produce residuals, standardised residuals and fitted values res2 <- residuals(model2) stres2 <- rstandard(model2) fits2 <- fitted(model2) # check model assumptions using plots plot(???) qqnorm(???) qqline(???) acf(???) What can you say about the assumptions of model2? All assumptions are being met Constant variance assumption is not met Zero mean assumption is not met Normality assumption is not met 5.4 Plot Data with Fitted Curve A plot of the data can be re-produced as before with the fitted curve added by obtaining the fitted values from model2 for each value of the variable dec.date in the data: ggplot(data = balaton, aes(x = dec.date, y = lswt)) + geom_line() + labs(title = "LSWT (Celsius) at Lake Balaton") + xlab("Date") + ylab("LSWT (Celcius)") + geom_line(aes(x = dec.date, y = fitted(model2)), col = 'red')
differencing.html
6 Differencing

6 Differencing An alternative approach to exploring the structure in the data is to use differencing. Differencing of order \(1\) was defined in lectures as: \[
abla X_t = X_t - X_{t-1} \] and, in general, differencing of order \(q\) can be written as: \[
abla^q X_t = 
abla[ 
abla^{q-1} X_t ] \] Differencing can be applied to a time series of observations, \(x\), in order to create a new series, \(z\); this method works well in removing trends, seasonal patterns and cycles. For a seasonal pattern with period \(d\), we can define the difference operation with lag \(d\) as follows: \[
abla_d X_t = X_t - X_{t-d} \] For example, first differencing \(q=1\) applied to a series with a linear trend eliminates the trend, while if there is a seasonal pattern in monthly data such that cycles of length 12 exist, a 12th difference, with lag \(d=12\), will remove them. To apply a lag 12 difference to the monthly LSWT data for Lake Balaton type: diff12 <- diff(lswt, lag = 12) We can then look at a plot of the differenced data obtained by: ggplot() + geom_line(aes(x = dec.date[-c(1:12)], y = diff12)) + xlab("Date") + ylab("Differenced Time Series") + geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", size = 0.5) In the above plot command, we remove the first 12 values of dec.date as they don't have corresponding values from diff12. The function geom_hline() in the above code plots a horizontal red (col = "red") line at zero (yintercept = 0). Has the lag 12 differencing successfully removed the seasonal pattern from the time series? No Yes An ACF of the differenced data can then be produced to check more formally whether or not the deterministic pattern (in this case the seasonal pattern) has been removed: acf(diff12, main="ACF of lag 12 differenced LSWT at Lake Balaton", xlab="Lag (months)") Does the above ACF of the de-seasonalised series highlight remaining serial autocorrelation? Yes No
plotting-spatial-data.html
7 Plotting Spatial Data

7 Plotting Spatial Data We are going to use the geoR library in R to look at an example of geostatistical data. library(geoR) The data we will look at corresponds to average rainfall over different years for the period May-June (dry-season). It was collected at 143 recording stations throughout Parana State, Brasil. Figure 7.1: Parana is one of the 26 states of Brazil, located in the South of the country. Image source: Wikipedia. First we need to load the data (which is stored within the geoR library in this case). data(parana) # rainfall measurements are stored in parana$data parana$data # location coordinates are stored in parana$coords parana$coords First we are going to plot the sampling locations. To get the proportions right for our plot we need to first ensure the plotting region is square. par(pty="s") plot(parana$coords) To look at the rainfall at each of the locations we can use the plot function. This produces a set of four plots describing the pattern of the data over space. plot(parana) Which of the following sentences best describes the pattern of rain across the space? Linear decreasing trend from north to south Higher rain in the east than in the west Higher rain in the south west than in the north east We can see from the top left plot that there is higher rain in the south west than in the north east.

8 Group Exercise 8.1 Once you have your dataset

8 Group Exercise Go to the website: http://shiny.maths-stats.gla.ac.uk/rhaggarty/S1ZLab/, and enter your favorite number between 1 and 600. This lake ID will generate a set of data based on lake mean surface water temperature (LSWT) measurements collected by the ArcLake project. The temperature measurements are in Kelvin. Press the download button and save the data on your PC. Open the data in R by first setting the working directory to where you have stored the data (go to Session > Set Working Directory > Choose Directory). Then open the data with the command: mydat <- read.csv("mydat.csv") 8.1 Once you have your dataset 8.1.1 Summarise the data by answering the following: Where is the lake located? (hint: use Google). How many observations are there? How many years of data are there? How many observations per year? 8.1.2 Use the Balaton data example to help with the R code needed to answer the following questions: Produce a time series plot and a plot of the seasonal pattern (boxplots for each time unit within a year). What are your subjective impressions from these plots? Fit a model with a trend and seasonal component. \[Y_t= \beta_0+\beta_1x_t+\gamma_1 \text{sin}\left(\frac{2\pi m_t}{p}\right)+\gamma_2 \text{cos}\left(\frac{2\pi m_t}{p}\right)+\epsilon_t\] Interpret the R output from the fitted model. Is there a statistically significant trend? Is there a statistically significant seasonal pattern? What does the R squared value tell you about the fitted model? Using the residuals from the fitted model, check the assumptions of: Independence (use an ACF plot) Normality Constant variance Zero mean
